item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
How is this not rated higher? It is brilliant.
+1 on so many levels.
In fact, +1 so hard that if in the future someone reads this comment and wants to discuss why this article rules, please give me a wikidot pm.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I didn't see this the first time I read it due to how much it changes, but at the end of the SCP-1471 look-alike document, the little button like thing1 is red. Is this by chance a reference to my wikiwalk edit where I linked 1471 to Capone in that spot, because it seems out of nowhere.

Other than that, I very much enjoyed this, I am a big fan of meta stuff, and this was no exception.

Footnotes
1. The one that says "Fuck"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I liked it. Truly an interesting article for obvious reasons. The Unknown tab led me to believe that the narrator was talking about himself (as either the villain or the Giver) and it felt just tiiiiiiiny bit like Nobody. The last part made me actually think that THAT was the document. I didn't connect it with 055, but I really like the secret

(quick question, how do I color text?)

at the end.

item.title: Re: Refresh...refresh..refresh
item.content:encoded:
I did not know about the minute wait time on getting new versions, after that it wasn't too hard to finally get the 1471 alt, and I kinda derped in not realising that tab four was the 055 alt.

Still, overall, a fun little ride that left me wanting to know more about what is going on. As seems to be the trend here in discussions: +1.

item.title: Re: Refresh...refresh..refresh
item.content:encoded:
I guess it's too obvious, then. :V

As far as I'm aware, the listpages module caches pages for 60 seconds, so if you refresh more frequently than that, it won't show you anything different. I will note that there is no alternate 055; that one's fixed. It's only the first three tabs that change.

You won't, however, miss a whole lot of the story by not seeing the extras. I tried to keep the permutations very similar, especially in the first tab, so you'd get the same experience reading it no matter which page came up.

item.title: Refresh...refresh..refresh
item.content:encoded:
So, the first time I looked at this I got 2682 and it sounded really familiar at first then I got what was going on. After I read tab 1 I skipped right on to the end to see the actual SCP to confirm my thoughts and I was right. Then went back to the other tabs in order. My other two were 2006 and 2991 which was kinda interesting to see how they got changed up but I really liked tab four and of course the fifth one was really the one I wanted to see more of. After reading though it all I slipped over into the discussion to see if there was anything more about it and found out there are good number of other articles to read.

So, I've been sitting here refreshing the page, and refreshing the page, and refreshing the page and I keep getting the same set of three over and over and over again. I've been ticking things off a list when I get them but so far I haven't been able to get the alternate 55 (which it seems a lot of people here in the discussion thinks is the best one) nor an alternate 1271 (which is the one I REALLY want to read since I just want to give SCP-1471-A a hug) but I've been at this refresh game for over an hour and am getting really tired of seeing the same set of three again and again.

It's a very neat idea, both the SCP itself and the random articles but it might just be best if you had them ALL show up in a bunch of tabs instead of only having three chosen at random. You have a story you want to tell, I want to read your story but if parts of the story are never shown to me I' can't read them.

As for the SCP itself it's a fun idea and I'm kinda curious as to this Giving Man and the "monster". My guess is Falzon was the Giving Man and 3245 is the "monster" but that kinda seems too obvious. Maybe I'm missing a key bit of information from one of the two alternative articles I've not been able to read. (Yes this is annoying me.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you! Also, this article is currently at +145, which makes me happy :3

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It really blends 1893 with 423, I feel.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really really enjoy this. I love the meta articles and I think this does an awesome job of conveying an original concept.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's too similar to Fred.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
spaghettification

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
i really like the idea, even though i first was really confused, it did turn out really nice!

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You really did not have to go to all that trouble, oh my god, I'm blushing. :O It is severely appreciated, you let me get a full night's sleep. XD

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ported to 3145 as requested. (Note to self: check the number of pages that comprise an SCP before starting to port it over.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"SUCK MY NUMEROUS DICKS

AT LEAST I THINK THESE ARE DICKS"

Yeah, this doesn't really feel like a 3000 SCP, but it's still pretty amusing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Very polished and well-crafted.

Unclear why this was a contest entry, since it's not remotely scary, nor does it really seem intended to be. (It could be, but only if the reader does all the work to getting there; sadness isn't the same as scary.)

Part of the problem with the interchangeable SCP rewrites is that they're… well, interchangeable, and it left me wondering why you didn't just use a test log, rather than a fancy format screw, besides the fancy format screw looking more impressive, which I suppose is fair enough. But also, you really could have done a lot more with the SCP rewrites, which are neither all that funny nor all that interesting once the novelty wears off.

I did like UNKNOWN DATA quite a bit. The hidden text itself was a bit on the nose in the phrasing and could use some tweaking.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: As others said, it's a great article, but doesn't fit the contest specs. I like the build up, but someone who is unfamiliar with the references articles wouldn't get this at all - might be something to think about.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Not really feeling this.

As others have pointed out upthread, entity trapped in a narrative has been done before, and this really doesn't break enough from the mold to make that aspect of the article shine. While some of the iterations got a good chuckle out of me, they really didn't leave much of an impact, and did not add much to the effect of horror.

Could it be improved? Certainly. There is plenty of fertile ground and directions that can be taken with the entity trapped in a narrative trope that could be played to create a horror article. As is, however, this article just doesn't do much.

-1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is good and I'll give it an upvote, but it's more humour than horror.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't think this has a place as 3000; the topic is horror, and this one just didn't make me feel any such thing. That said, I like the concept, and I'd be lying if I said I didn't snort when 76 was the first thing to come up and I realized what I was about to read. It's a cliched topic, but it's an amusing one.

I'll be withholding my vote for now as again, I don't think this is 3000-worthy, but if it gets polished up and re-numbered after the contest is over, throw me a bone and I'll give it another read.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I wish I could've seen this as a draft. It's a creative approach, and I can get behind the story (not so much the characters, more on that later), but for me, the execution was botched on a few fronts.

Alright, so the first doc I came across was for -76. I read the entire thing, and despite you making some minor changes as to not directly lift content, I couldn't tell the difference - it may as well have been copy-pasted. This goes for basically everything else aside from -055. I just read a ton of material I've already seen before!

The interviews themselves are repetitive. I got it after the first trio - I don't know if it was necessary to do a dozen when they almost all have the same material ( 1. I'm a shitty murdering prick. 2. Yes you are, and I hate you.). That was the second strike for me.

Now, all the exposition comes pouring in across the 055/unknown docs, as well as us finally accessing the basic details of the skip. It's far too much, all at once, and it's unsatisfying and comes across as heavy-handed.

I'd like to suggest, despite the current surviving status of the skip, a significant rewrite. I think the backstory of the pair, what they mean to each other and how they interact, could instead be spread through the various documents - similar to how you executed 055's. There is so much wasted opportunity to work in small humiliations and punishments to 3K, as well as retaliations from it in both the SCProcedures and description. There's opportunity to actually characterize the pair in how they act - defining them by what it is they do in the narratives instead of simply talking shit.

I think you're leaning on the interview crutch too much (MAl0 aside, that actually made sense). It seems contrived for SCPs which don't include said interviews - I think the individual articles would be stronger if they held the structure of the original.

Spreading that exposition out actually gives you more to say in each iteration of the document. It was a chore to actually access everything, and unlike (for example) the minotaur (which has literal verbatim text repeated in each iteration) nothing past the first couple felt fresh. Opening these up with more hints as to what's happened/what is going on gives readers something to hunt for and look forward to as they piece together the puzzle you lay before them. If this were the case, I'd be interested in reading every damn one to get the full experience - as it stands, I scrolled past a bunch of regurgitated content to access a few contrived, repetitive chunks of dialogue.

Downvote because I think this could have been so much better.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I will freely admit that I have done my best to wring horror from this article, but it was not originally conceived with horror as an objective, so not the greatest, as contest entries go. If you want to no-vote or downvote now and reconsider after the contest, I will be fine with that. :)

item.title: Re: Now that all the flailing is over
item.content:encoded: I did not say third page, sirpudding. :B I did not.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I actually didn't know Mnestics were a thing, and yeah, as West said I through it was a typo.

Thanks for the correction!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Context should make the meaning clear. Mnesi- "remembering" is stemmed from mnasthai "to remember"; with the - tic suffix for medical agents it becomes mnestic. Which therefore is a drug that induces remembering. Adding the a- prefix would negate this and therefore amnestic would be a drug that induces forgetting. Since in the context the subjects are being expected to remember something, a drug that induces memory makes sense.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Maybe he didn't know that mnestics were a thing already?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Why? Mnestics make sense here in context, since they are trying to get them to remember something. I would think that amnestics would be contraindicated.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Blur thought mnestics was a typo.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Pointing what out?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: mnestics are a thing. http://www.scp-wiki.net/search:site/q/mnestic

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Just pointing it out:

Individuals interacting with SCP-3000 are unable to provide any details about its identity to third parties, even with application of mnestics.

Anyways, really good article. I honestly feel terrible for SCP-3000. But it didn't really scared me so.. yeah, withholding vote until the contest is over.

Edit: TIL mnestics are a thing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think the others pretty much said everything there is to say about why this may not fit the contest's theme, but I like the story and the ideas here (e.g. having everything taken away from you to the point where all that is left of you is your very existence) too much to ignore this article.

I'll definitely upvote this when the contest is over.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well, that went from funny to depressing fast.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I have to agree with sirpudding & psul-as an entry, it was fun to read, but as a 3000 option, I would have to pass. I certainly didn't get any sense of horror at any time. I would upvote if it popped up as a new regular SCP entry, due to the breadth of the cross-contamination and the playfulness thereof. Novote until then.

edit: and with the move to a new slot, +1!

item.title: Re: Now that all the flailing is over
item.content:encoded:
but oh boy, there aren't a lot of Series III on that list.

On the third page and after there are a few Series III articles.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I feel much the same. I can respect the amount of work put into this, especially after having refreshed and read through a few additional iterations. But it didn't scare me, and I agree with sirpudding that the "entity trapped in narrative" wasn't used in a surprising way. The inviso-text also doesn't work on mobile for me, meaning that I had to guess what was going on, and come back to it on another screen.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't know how I feel about this. We have seen "entity trapped in narrative" in at least four articles I can think of already, and this does a few things to distinguish itself a bit, but really doesn't do enough to feel genuinely original in this regard. I also wasn't especially scared here, and while this has horror elements, I don't think it is successful horror. It was a very entertaining and well-crafted read, though. I think I am no-voting and will return after the contest to reevaluate this.

item.title: Now that all the flailing is over
item.content:encoded:
I swear to god if this gets downvoted I am just going to die

Well, here it is at long last. I went all out for this contest, with my most ambitious article to date, and I really hope people get something out of it, because I've been severely pessimistic about my ability to write skips for the last year or… five.

Many, many, many thanks to anqxyr for writing the ListPages code for this, helping me figure out how to get my ideas to work on Wikidot, and a 0 hour hotfix. Thanks as well to Tuomey, Aiden Eldritch and A Random Day for further tech assist.

Feedback thanks goes to Joreth, ARD and djkaktus. I hope I've done their suggestions justice.

Further thanks involves spoilers, so do consider going back and refreshing a few times before you head to the collapsible.

+ Credits and spoilers
– hide block

Original authors were contacted for permission when feasible. I have done my level best to rewrite the articles in my own words — no copy-pasting here, I promise! Some of these were more work than others. (Rewriting 173 to modern standards is the hardest thing I have ever done on this site.) Credit as follows:

SCP-049, Plague Doctor, by Gabriel Jade
SCP-055, [unkown], by qntm
SCP-076, "Able", by Kain Pathos Crow
SCP-087, The Stairwell, by Zaeyde
SCP-106, The Old Man, by Dr Gears
SCP-173, The Statue, by Moto42
SCP-610, The Flesh That Hates, by NekoChris
SCP-682, Hard-to-Destroy Reptile, by Dr Gears
SCP-1048, Builder Bear, by trennerdios
SCP-1171, Humans Go Home, by DrEverettMann
SCP-1193, Buried Giant, by ophite
SCP-1440, The Old Man from Nowhere, by Dmatix
SCP-1471, MalO ver1.0.0, by LurkD
SCP-2006, Too Spooky, by weizhong
SCP-2682, The Blind Idiot, by Faminepulse
SCP-2991, "Scarf", by Zyn and Dr Hysteria

Articles were chosen from the highest-rated pages. I did my best to include articles from Series II and III, but oh boy, there aren't a lot of Series III on that list. (2521 was too hard to work with. :C) But there's at least one article from each Series in each subset of the first three tabs. And if you didn't notice, tab 4 is always 055, it's only the first three that change.

I hope I don't have to say it, but this isn't meant as a 'take that' to any of the featured articles, no matter how much I rewrote them. Any commentary is in-character for 3000, and 3000 is kind of a huge dick.

One more big thank-you to SunnyClockwork for the image. I literally told her "draw me something", and what a something she came up with! :D
